roadmaps.title: Proofcraft — the path
roadmaps.description: An original progression through the standard foundations of proof: logic, sets, functions, relations, induction, and number theory.

render.text: What is a proof? In mathematics, a proof is an argument that establishes the validity (truth) of an assertion. Unlike a courtroom or a science experiment, a mathematical proof leaves no room for doubt.

sources.source_quote: An argument that establishes the validity (truth) of an assertion.

render.text: Why is checking a few examples (e.g.
) not a proof that the sum of two evens is even?

options.text: A proof must somehow test all possible examples simultaneously, not just a few.
The handful of examples chosen were not genuinely even numbers.
Examples are merely illustrations and are never allowed in a proof.
Addition is not actually well defined when both inputs are even.

solution.text: Testing examples can build intuition, but for a proof we somehow need to test all possible examples simultaneously — which we do by arguing from the definition.

sources.source_quote: we somehow need to test all possible examples simultaneously.

render.text: Put the proof that the sum of two even integers is even in the correct order.

steps.text: Let x and y be even integers.
By the definition of even, write x = 2k and y = 2l for some integers k and l.
Add them: x + y = 2k + 2l = 2(k + l).
Since k + l is an integer, x + y has the form 2(integer), so x + y is even.

render.text: Theorems vs. conjectures. Theorems are true mathematical statements that we can prove. In practice we are often confronted with conjectures: statements we suspect to be true, but which we don't (yet) know how to prove.

render.text: True or false: once a conjecture has been proved, it becomes a theorem.

solution.text: True. A conjecture is a statement we suspect to be true but cannot yet prove; theorems are true mathematical statements that we can prove. Proving a conjecture turns it into a theorem.

sources.source_quote: Theorems are true mathematical statements that we can prove.

render.text: Definition (proposition). A proposition (or statement) is a sentence that is either true or false.

render.text: Understanding the distinction between the converse and contrapositive is vital: the contrapositive is logically equivalent to the original, but the converse need not be.

solution.text: Example: “if Claudia has a peach, she has a fruit” is true; its converse “if she has a fruit, she has a peach” is false, yet its contrapositive “if she has no fruit, she has no peach” is true.

render.text: Order Euclid’s proof by contradiction that there are infinitely many prime numbers.

steps.text: Assume there are exactly n primes p₁, …, pₙ.
Define the integer Π = p₁ · … · pₙ + 1.
Π is divisible by some prime pᵢ on the list, as is the product p₁ · … · pₙ.
Then their difference, 1 = Π − p₁ · … · pₙ, must also be divisible by pᵢ.
But pᵢ ≥ 2 cannot divide 1: contradiction. So there are infinitely many primes.

render.text: Order the steps of the Euclidean algorithm to find

steps.text: Divide a by b: write a = q₁b + r₁ with 0 ≤ r₁ < b.
If the last remainder is positive, divide the previous divisor by it (e.g. b = q₂r₁ + r₂).
Keep repeating the division algorithm, dividing each remainder into the one before it.
Stop once a remainder of zero appears.
Report the last non-zero remainder as gcd(a, b).

render.text: Put the steps of a standard induction proof of a statement

steps.md: Set-up: state “We prove by induction” and define the proposition $P(n)$.
Base case: prove that $P(1)$ is true.
Induction step: fix $n$ and assume the induction hypothesis $P(n)$.
Use $P(n)$ to deduce that $P(n+1)$ is true.
Conclude: by induction, $P(n)$ holds for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$.

solution.text: An induction proof runs set-up, base case, induction step (assume
), and finally the conclusion. The result is an infinite chain of implications

sources.source_quote: The result is an infinite chain of implications

render.text: Order the steps to prove Theorem 6.7: if

steps.text: Suppose A ⊆ B and let C ∈ P(A); we must show C ∈ P(B).
By definition of power set, C ∈ P(A) means C ⊆ A.
Since C ⊆ A and A ⊆ B, transitivity gives C ⊆ B.
By definition of power set, C ⊆ B means C ∈ P(B).
Hence every C ∈ P(A) lies in P(B), so P(A) ⊆ P(B).

render.text: Order the steps proving that the equivalence classes of

steps.text: By reflexivity, x ∼ x, so x ∈ [x]; hence every x ∈ X lies in some equivalence class (the classes cover X).
To show classes are disjoint, suppose [x] ∩ [y] ≠ ∅, so pick z in both.
Then z ∼ x and z ∼ y; by symmetry x ∼ z, and by transitivity x ∼ y.
By the lemma x ∼ y ⇒ [x] = [y], so any two classes that meet are equal — distinct classes are disjoint.

render.text: Order the steps of Cantor's diagonal argument showing

steps.text: Suppose, for contradiction, that g : N → (0,1) is a bijection.
List the values g(1), g(2), g(3), … as decimals 0.b_{n1}b_{n2}b_{n3}…
Build a new decimal x whose nth digit disagrees with the nth digit of g(n).
Then x ≠ g(n) for every n, so x is not in the list.
But x ∈ (0,1) and g is surjective, so x must be in the list — contradiction.

with the diagonal of the list. Since

render.text: CR01. Three students hand in proofs that the sum of an odd integer and an even integer is odd. Which proof is valid (correct in both logic and exposition)?

options.text: Proof 1: a string of equations with no words, e.g. $x+y=(2k+1)+2j=\dots=2(k+j)+1$, with no statement of what $k,j$ are.
Proof 2: 'Let $x=2k+1$ and $y=2k$ for some $k$.' — reusing the same $k$ for both forces $y$ to depend on $x$.
Proof 3 (repaired): Let $x=2k+1$ and $y=2j$ for integers $k,j$. Then $x+y=2(k+j)+1$, which is odd.

There is no error at all; the proof is completely correct as written.

The range of the function.

solution.text: The pre-image of V is the set of inputs which are mapped into V; (b) describes the image.

sources.source_quote: Inverse image (or pre-image) of a subset V ⊆B: the set of inputs which are mapped into V

options.text: f(a1) = f(a2) implies a1 = a2.
For every b there is some a with f(a) = b.
a1 = a2 implies f(a1) = f(a2).
f(a1) is never equal to f(a2).

solution.text: Injective: equal outputs force equal inputs. (b) is surjectivity; (c) holds for any function; (d) is false.

sources.source_quote: Injective (1–1, an injection) if distinct inputs produce distinct outputs.

concepts.title: What is a proof?
concepts.blurb: A proof is an argument that establishes the truth of an assertion by reference to definitions.

concepts.title: Even integers
concepts.blurb: An integer is even if it can be written as 2k for some integer k.

concepts.title: Sum of two evens is even
concepts.blurb: The sum of any pair of even integers is even; the standard proof rearranges the definition.

concepts.title: Theorems and conjectures
concepts.blurb: A theorem is a proved statement; a conjecture is one we suspect but cannot yet prove.

concepts.title: Counterexample
concepts.blurb: A single instance where a claim fails disproves a statement made about all cases.

concepts.title: Proposition
concepts.blurb: A statement that is either true or false.

concepts.title: Connectives: and / or / not
concepts.blurb: Conjunction, disjunction, and negation combine propositions.

concepts.title: Tautology and contradiction
concepts.blurb: A tautology is always true; a contradiction is always false.

concepts.title: Conditional and biconditional
concepts.blurb: P implies Q (hypothesis, conclusion); P iff Q.

concepts.title: Converse and contrapositive
concepts.blurb: Converse reverses a conditional; contrapositive is equivalent to it.

concepts.title: Propositional function
concepts.blurb: A family of propositions depending on a variable.

concepts.title: Universal and existential quantifiers
concepts.blurb: For-all and there-exists over a domain.

concepts.title: Negating quantifiers
concepts.blurb: Negation swaps for-all and there-exists, negating the inside.

concepts.title: Direct proof
concepts.blurb: Assume the hypothesis P and deduce the conclusion Q; the simplest logical flow.

concepts.title: Proof by contrapositive
concepts.blurb: Directly prove ¬Q ⇒ ¬P, which is logically equivalent to P ⇒ Q.

concepts.title: Proof by contradiction
concepts.blurb: Assume P and ¬Q, then derive a contradiction to conclude P ⇒ Q.

concepts.title: Choosing a proof strategy
concepts.blurb: How to pick among direct, contrapositive, and contradiction approaches.

concepts.title: Rational and irrational numbers
concepts.blurb: x is rational if x = m/n for integers m, n; irrational if no such integers exist.

concepts.title: Prime numbers
concepts.blurb: An integer ≥ 2 is prime if its only positive divisors are itself and 1.

concepts.title: Divisibility
concepts.blurb: n | m means m = nk for some integer k.

concepts.title: Division algorithm
concepts.blurb: Every m = qn + r with a unique quotient q and remainder 0 ≤ r < n.

concepts.title: Congruence modulo n
concepts.blurb: a ≡ b (mod n) when a and b share the same remainder on division by n.

concepts.title: Greatest common divisor
concepts.blurb: gcd(a, b) is the largest positive divisor of both a and b.

concepts.title: Euclidean algorithm
concepts.blurb: Repeated division; gcd(a, b) is the last non-zero remainder.

concepts.title: Bézout's identity
concepts.blurb: There exist integers x, y with gcd(a, b) = ax + by.

concepts.title: Union of sets
concepts.blurb: A ∪ B is the set of elements lying in A or in B (or in both).

concepts.title: Intersection of sets
concepts.blurb: A ∩ B is the set of elements lying in both A and B; disjoint means A ∩ B = ∅.

concepts.title: Complement of a set
concepts.blurb: Aᶜ is the set of elements of a universal set U not in A; B \ A is the relative complement.

concepts.title: Function (domain & codomain)
concepts.blurb: f : A → B assigns each input a ∈ A a single output f(a) ∈ B; A is the domain, B the codomain.

concepts.title: Image, range & pre-image
concepts.blurb: f(U) is the set of outputs from inputs in U; range f = f(A); f⁻¹(V) is the inverse image of V.

concepts.title: Injective, surjective, bijective
concepts.blurb: Injective: distinct inputs give distinct outputs; surjective: every output is realized; bijective: both.

concepts.title: Proof by induction
concepts.blurb: Prove a base case and an induction step to establish a proposition for every natural number.

concepts.title: Induction hypothesis
concepts.blurb: In the induction step, the assumed proposition P(n) used to deduce P(n+1).

concepts.title: Well-ordering principle
concepts.blurb: Every non-empty subset of N has a minimum element; this justifies induction.

concepts.title: Strong induction
concepts.blurb: An induction step may assume all previous propositions; may need several base cases.

concepts.title: Cartesian product
concepts.blurb: A × B is the set of all ordered pairs (a, b) with a ∈ A and b ∈ B.

concepts.title: Power set
concepts.blurb: P(A) is the set of all subsets of A; if |A| = n then |P(A)| = 2^n.

concepts.title: Indexed unions and intersections
concepts.blurb: For a collection {An}, the union collects x in some An and the intersection collects x in every An.

concepts.title: Binary relation
concepts.blurb: A relation R from A to B is a set of ordered pairs, i.e. a subset of A × B.

concepts.title: Reflexive, symmetric, transitive
concepts.blurb: Three properties a relation on a set may satisfy: x ∼ x; x ∼ y ⇒ y ∼ x; x ∼ y and y ∼ z ⇒ x ∼ z.

concepts.title: Equivalence relation
concepts.blurb: A relation that is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.

concepts.title: Equivalence class
concepts.blurb: The set [x] of all elements related to x under an equivalence relation.

concepts.title: Partition
concepts.blurb: A collection of non-empty subsets that cover X and are pairwise disjoint.

concepts.title: Equivalence classes partition
concepts.blurb: The equivalence classes of an equivalence relation form a partition, and vice versa.

concepts.title: Cardinality
concepts.blurb: An abstract measure of size; for finite sets it is the number of elements.

concepts.title: Same cardinality (bijection)
concepts.blurb: |A| = |B| means there is a bijection between A and B.

concepts.title: Finite and infinite sets
concepts.blurb: A finite set has finitely many elements; an infinite set can match a proper subset.

concepts.title: Countably infinite sets
concepts.blurb: A set is countably infinite when it is in bijection with the natural numbers (cardinality aleph-nought).

concepts.title: Aleph-nought as least infinite cardinal
concepts.blurb: Every infinite set has cardinality at least that of the natural numbers.

concepts.title: Uncountable sets
concepts.blurb: A set is uncountable when its cardinality strictly exceeds aleph-nought; e.g. the interval (0,1).

concepts.title: Cantor–Schröder–Bernstein
concepts.blurb: Two injections, one each way, force a bijection, so the cardinalities are equal.

concepts.title: Problem-set practice
concepts.blurb: Mixed practice from MATH 314 homework & concept-reinforcement sheets.
